https://lifeisacanvas24.github.io/.date:
"Life is a Canvas" aims to be your go-to source of inspiration and motivation, catering to the vibrant community of vegans and those aspiring to embrace veganism. Whether you're passionate about travel, savoring clean and wholesome plant-based delights, or exploring various aspects of life, we've got you covered.
Our content spans across travel adventures, lifestyle insights, parenting tips, promoting compassion towards all living beings, spiritual explorations, yoga, engaging workshops, and noteworthy events worldwide. If there's a specific topic you'd love us to delve into, don't hesitate to shoot us a message.
A heartfelt thank you to each one of you for visiting "Life is a Canvas." Together, let's paint our journey just the way we like it!

https://lifeisacanvas24.github.io/blog/t.body:
[open_graph]
title = "Comprehensive GitHub Workflow Guide"
description = "A detailed guide on setting up and managing GitHub repositories, including best practices"
image = ""
url = "http://lifeisacanvas24.github.io/blog/technology/comprehensive-github-workflow-guide/"
type = "post"
Comprehensive GitHub Workflow GuideTable of ContentsSetting up a repository on GitHubSetting up SSH accessCloning and setting up the local repositoryWorking with branchesManaging Pull RequestsManaging conflicts and diffsKeeping branches up to dateRemoving and resyncing a branchBest Practices and Advanced TechniquesDeep Dive: Git Stash and Git RebaseCommitting from an Existing Folder to GitHub1. Setting up a repository on GitHubLog in to your GitHub accountClick the "+" icon in the top right corner and select "New repository"Fill in the repository name and descriptionChoose public or privateSelect "Initialize this repository with a README"Click "Create repository"2. Setting up SSH accessCheck for existing SSH keys:Generate a new SSH key (if needed):or for custom keyname you could useStart the SSH agent:Add your SSH key to the agent:or for custom keyname you could useCopy the SSH public key:or for custom keyname you could useAdd the SSH key to your GitHub account:Go to GitHub Settings > SSH and GPG keys > New SSH keyPaste your key and saveTest your SSH connection:3. Cloning and setting up the local repositoryClone the repository:Create multiple branches:4. Working with branchesSwitch to a branch:Make changes and commit:Push changes to a specific branch:5. Managing Pull RequestsCreate a pull request:Go to your repository on GitHubClick "Pull requests" tab > "New pull request"Select the base and compare branchesAdd a title and descriptionClick "Create pull request"Merge a pull request:Review the changesClick "Merge pull request" if everything looks goodClick "Confirm merge"6. Managing conflicts and diffsResolve conflicts:If GitHub indicates conflicts, click "Resolve conflicts"Edit the file(s) to resolve conflicts manuallyMark the conflicts as resolvedCommit the changesView diffs:In a pull request, go to the "Files changed" tabOr locally:7. Keeping branches up to dateUpdate local master:Update feature branch:8. Removing and resyncing a branchDelete a branch locally:Delete a branch on GitHub:Resync a branch from scratch:9. Best Practices and Advanced Techniques9.1 Always pull the latest changes before starting work9.2 Create feature branches for new work9.3 Keep commits atomic and write meaningful commit messages9.4 Regularly merge master into your feature branch9.5 Use pull requests for code reviewPush your feature branch:Create a pull request on GitHubRequest reviews from team membersAddress feedback and make necessary changesMerge the pull request when approved9.6 Delete feature branches after mergingLocally:On GitHub:9.7 Use meaningful branch names9.8 Commit often, perfect later, publish once9.9 Use git stash for temporary changes9.10 Use git rebase for a cleaner history9.11 Skip certain files from being committedCreate or edit .gitignore file in your repository root:Add patterns to ignore:

Ignore the build directory
/build/
Ignore a specific file
config.ini
Ignore all files in a directory
temp/*
But don't ignore this specific file
!temp/important.txtCommit the .gitignore file:If you've already committed files that you now want to ignore:Then commit this change.9.12 Use git aliases for common commandsAdd to your ~/.gitconfig:9.13 Use interactive rebase to clean up commits before pushing9.14 Use git hooks for automated checksCreate a pre-commit hook:Edit the pre-commit hook to run tests or linters:9.15 Use git blame to understand code history9.16 Use git bisect to find bugs10. Deep Dive: Git Stash and Git Rebase10.1 Git StashGit stash is a powerful feature that allows you to temporarily store modified, tracked files in order to clean your working directory without committing incomplete work.Use Cases for Git Stash:When you need to quickly switch branches but aren't ready to commit your current work.When you want to apply a stash to multiple branches.When you need to put aside some experimental changes.Common Git Stash Commands:Stash your changes:List all stashes:Apply the most recent stash and remove it from the stash list:Apply a specific stash without removing it:Create a new branch from a stash:Remove a specific stash:Clear all stashes:Example Workflow:10.2 Git RebaseGit rebase is a way to integrate changes from one branch into another. It's an alternative to merging that can result in a cleaner, more linear project history.Use Cases for Git Rebase:Keeping a feature branch up-to-date with the latest changes from the main branch.Cleaning up your local commit history before pushing to a shared repository.Maintaining a linear project history.Common Git Rebase Commands:Basic rebase:Interactive rebase (for editing, squashing, or reordering commits):Continue a rebase after resolving conflicts:Abort a rebase:Example Workflow:Interactive Rebase Example:Important Notes:Rebasing rewrites commit history. Never rebase commits that have been pushed to a public repository unless you're absolutely sure no one has based work on them.When using git push --force-with-lease, be cautious as it can overwrite remote changes. It's safer than git push --force as it checks if the remote branch has been updated.Stashing is local to your machine. Stashes are not transferred to the remote repository when you push.Always communicate with your team about your Git workflow, especially when using advanced features like rebase.11. Committing from an Existing Folder to GitHubIf you have an existing folder with files that you want to commit to a new or existing GitHub repository, follow these steps:11.1 Initialize a New RepositoryNavigate to your existing folder:Initialize a new Git repository:Add the files to the staging area:Commit your changes:11.2 Linking to a Remote RepositoryAdd the remote repository (replace <username> and <repository> with your actual GitHub username and repository name):Push your changes to GitHub:11.3 If the Repository Already Exists on GitHubIf you have an existing GitHub repository and want to commit files from your local folder:Clone the repository to a local folder (optional, if you don't want to work directly in the existing folder):Copy or move your existing files into this cloned repository folder.Add, commit, and push your changes as shown above:11.4 Managing Future ChangesAfter committing from an existing folder, manage future changes by:Creating a branch for new features:Making changes, adding, and committing:Pushing to GitHub:Creating a pull request on GitHub to merge your changes into the main branch.ConclusionThis guide provides a comprehensive overview of GitHub workflows, including essential commands, best practices, and advanced techniques. Regularly updating your knowledge of Git and GitHub will enhance your development workflow, enabling effective collaboration and version control.Feel free to refer back to this guide as needed, and happy coding!
https://lifeisacanvas24.github.io/blog/t.description: A detailed guide on setting up and managing GitHub repositories, including best practices.
https://lifeisacanvas24.github.io/blog/t.title: Comprehensive GitHub Workflow Guide

https://lifeisacanvas24.github.io/blog/t.body:
Open-source chat, project management, CRM, meetings, email, documentation, support, data visualization, automation, backends, and an AI chatbot platform.
Hi there, if you are running a startup or planning to start one, you're in the right place. In this article, I will discuss open-source SaaS products that every startup needs. I have curated this list to cover almost all the necessary platforms to ensure smooth operations.
Usually, startups require tools for chat, project management, CRM, online meetings, email, documentation, customer support, data visualization, workflow automation, backends, and even an AI chatbot platform. Here's a list of well-known open-source alternatives for each of these essential tools:
1. Rocket.Chat for Chat
An open-source alternative to Slack, Microsoft Teams, and Discord.
As we all know, communication between team members is critical to keep operations running smoothly. Rocket.Chat provides excellent solutions to facilitate seamless communication. It helps boost productivity and customer satisfaction.
Features:

Real-time messaging
Cross-platform integrations
Omnichannel support
Video and voice calls
End-to-end encryption
Self-hosting
User management
Custom notifications

...
https://lifeisacanvas24.github.io/blog/t.description: Open-source chat, project management, CRM, meetings, email, documentation, support, data visualization, automation, backends, and an AI chatbot platform.
https://lifeisacanvas24.github.io/blog/t.title: Top 11 Open Source SaaS Alternatives That Every Startup Needs
